title: Minimize access to the proxy sub-resource of nodes

input_row.title: Minimize access to the proxy sub-resource of nodes
input_row.description: Users with access to the Proxy sub-resource of Node objects automatically have permissions to use the kubelet API, which may allow for privilege escalation or bypass cluster security controls such as audit logs. The kubelet provides an API which includes rights to execute commands in any container running on the node. Access to this API is covered by permissions to the main Kubernetes API via the node object. The proxy sub-resource specifically allows wide ranging access to the kubelet API. Direct access to the kubelet API bypasses controls like audit logging (there is no audit log of kubelet API access) and admission control.
input_row.rationale: The ability to use the proxy sub-resource of node objects opens up possibilities for privilege escalation and should be restricted, where possible.
input_row.audit: Review the users who have access to the proxy sub-resource of node objects in the Kubernetes API.
input_row.remediation: Where possible, remove access to the proxy sub-resource of node objects. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/rbac-good-practices/#access-to- proxy-subresource-of-nodes 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/kubelet-authn- authz/#kubelet-authorization

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_rbac_node_proxy_subresource_access_restricted

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- **Objective:** Identify all Kubernetes RBAC (Role and ClusterRole) bindings that grant access to the proxy sub-resource of Node objects (i.e., "nodes/proxy" verbs).
- **Kubernetes API calls / kubectl commands:**
  - List all ClusterRoles and Roles:
    - `kubectl get clusterroles -o yaml`
    - `kubectl get roles --all-namespaces -o yaml`
  - Search for rules with:
    - `apiGroups: [""]`
    - `resources: ["nodes/proxy"]` or `resources: ["nodes"]` with `verbs` including `proxy`
  - List all ClusterRoleBindings and RoleBindings:
    - `kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o yaml`
    - `kubectl get rolebindings --all-namespaces -o yaml`
  - Map which users, groups, or service accounts are bound to these roles.
- **Configuration properties/settings to validate:**
  - Any Role or ClusterRole that grants access to "nodes/proxy" (resource) with any verb (especially "get", "create", "update", "delete", "proxy").
  - Any binding (RoleBinding or ClusterRoleBinding) that attaches such a role to a user, group, or service account.
- **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
  - **Pass:** No users, groups, or service accounts are granted access to the "nodes/proxy" sub-resource except for the minimal set required for cluster operation (ideally, only system components if absolutely necessary).
  - **Fail:** Any non-system user, group, or service account is granted access to the "nodes/proxy" sub-resource.
- **Example check logic:**
  1. Enumerate all Roles/ClusterRoles with rules granting access to "nodes/proxy".
  2. Enumerate all RoleBindings/ClusterRoleBindings referencing those roles.
  3. Output all subjects (users, groups, service accounts) with such access for review/remediation.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.11 PDF
